title: Mixed-Use Portfolio Manager
setting: A portfolio spanning office, retail, and shared public-realm or amenity components
companySize: Multi-asset institutional or major private owner platform
description: You manage several assets with different tenant types, service rhythms, and owner expectations. The role is less about one building's daily detail and more about pattern recognition, risk scanning, client storytelling, and making sure each site is run to a consistent standard without losing local judgment.
salary: Usually higher than single-asset roles because of broader scope, more client exposure, and multi-site accountability.

schedule.activity: Portfolio scan of incidents, occupancy changes, arrears exceptions, and major work orders across all assets
schedule.note: Portfolio work starts with finding where attention is needed most today.

schedule.activity: Call with on-site property managers to reset priorities and escalate one risk item from each asset
schedule.note: A good portfolio manager creates focus, not extra noise.

schedule.activity: Review quarterly client-report draft covering NOI movement, leasing exposure, capex needs, and ESG progress
schedule.note: Portfolio reporting is about trend and strategy, not just activity lists.

schedule.activity: Meeting with asset management on renewal risk, vacancy planning, and repositioning priorities
schedule.note: This is where property management becomes a strategic advisory function.

schedule.activity: Review contractor performance and procurement strategy across multiple service lines
schedule.note: Portfolio scale can unlock savings, but only if service quality stays visible.

schedule.activity: ESG check-in on metering gaps, utility trends, and proposed efficiency projects across the portfolio
schedule.note: Portfolio sustainability work is partly about standardization and partly about asset-specific judgment.

schedule.activity: Owner briefing on one asset's capex decision, another asset's tenant retention risk, and portfolio-level operating themes
schedule.note: Senior clients value managers who can compare assets clearly and recommend where to act first.

schedule.activity: Document decisions, reset watch-list items, and align tomorrow's asks for site teams
schedule.note: Portfolio leadership depends on turning many moving parts into a calm operating rhythm.

responsibilities: Oversee performance, risk, and reporting across multiple asset types
Coach on-site managers while maintaining portfolio standards
Prepare quarterly and strategic client reviews
Prioritize capex, renewal, and service interventions across assets
Monitor portfolio-wide contractor, ESG, and compliance patterns
Translate site-level activity into owner-level decisions
challenges: Attention can fragment quickly across competing asset issues
Different asset types need different service instincts and metrics
Client expectations often rise as scope expands
It is easy to lose site reality if reporting becomes too abstract
You must lead through other managers, not just through direct action
rewards: You get a broader commercial view of how property performance is created
The work builds strong client, portfolio, and leadership capability
You gain experience close to asset management and strategic planning
There is more room to shape standards and systems, not just respond to issues
It is one of the clearest pathways into senior property leadership
careerPath: Senior property manager or client-facing lead
Head of portfolio operations or major-account lead
Director of property management, account leadership, or owner-side advisory
